column2: Concise definition
column3: Typical observable behaviors
column4: Psychological function + short-term benefits
column5: Long-term costs/harms
column6: Typical origins/etiology (developmental, attachment, cognitive)
column7: Prevalence / population notes
column8: Strength of evidence + key references
column9: Evidence-based interventions (brief; support level)

column2: Reducing distress by escaping triggers (situations, sensations, tasks, emotions).
column3: Procrastination; canceling/withdrawing; “numbing” via screens/substances; conflict avoidance.
column4: Fn: immediate anxiety relief, preserves perceived safety/control. ST: lowers arousal quickly.
column5: Reinforces fear; reduced mastery; narrowed life; depression-maintaining inactivity.
column6: Learning history of negative reinforcement; threat sensitization; low tolerance for affect; anxiety/depression cycles.
column7: Procrastination is extremely common in students (estimates 80–95%); adult chronic procrastination often ~20% in survey work.
column8: Strong (avoidance in anxiety maintenance; transdiagnostic models).
column9: CBT exposure/behavioral experiments (strong for anxiety). ACT (psychological flexibility; strong/moderate overall). Behavioral activation for avoidance/inactivity (strong for depression).

column1: Safety behaviors & compulsive checking / reassurance seeking
column2: Behaviors used to prevent feared outcomes or reduce doubt (often anxiety/OCD).
column3: Checking, “just in case” rituals; reassurance questions; avoidance plus “protective” props; scanning body/symptoms.
column4: Fn: reduces uncertainty, transfers responsibility, prevents feared catastrophe. ST: rapid relief.
column5: Maintains threat beliefs; increases dependence; relationship strain; can generalize into broader control rituals.
column6: Intolerance of uncertainty; inflated responsibility; learning via relief; anxious attachment in interpersonal reassurance.
column7: Common across anxiety/OCD; exact prevalence varies by measure; interpersonal reassurance seeking noted across OCD/depression/anxiety.
column8: Moderate–strong (conceptual and empirical support; meta-analytic debate on whether to fully remove safety behaviors during exposure).
column9: Exposure with response prevention / exposure-based CBT (strong). Inhibitory-learning-informed exposure with strategic fading of safety behaviors (moderate; nuanced).

column2: Repetitive dwelling on negative mood, causes, meaning, or self-evaluation.
column3: “Replay” conversations; circular analysis; difficulty disengaging; sleep disruption.
column4: Fn: attempts to solve/understand; signal importance; avoids action/uncertainty. ST: sense of control/meaning.
column5: Predicts/maintains depression and anxiety; impairs sleep and problem-solving; social withdrawal.
column6: Learned cognitive habit; self-criticism; trauma shame; metacognitive beliefs (“I must think this through”).
column7: Transdiagnostic and common in both clinical and nonclinical samples.
column8: Strong (meta-analytic links to anxiety/depression; RNT as transdiagnostic construct).
column9: CBT targeting RNT (moderate–strong). MBCT for relapse prevention (strong for recurrent depression). Rumination-focused CBT (emerging–moderate).

column2: Persistent verbal thinking about possible future threats/uncertainty.
column3: Excessive “what if” planning; reassurance seeking; over-preparing; sleep-onset delay.
column4: Fn: threat simulation; prepares/avoids regret. ST: sense of preparedness.
column5: Maintains anxiety; perpetuates intolerance of uncertainty; reduces present-moment engagement.
column6: Trait vulnerability (neuroticism), IU beliefs, learned safety strategy.
column7: Common across anxiety and mixed emotional disorder samples.
column8: Strong (RNT framework; transdiagnostic evidence).
column9: CBT for worry/RNT (strong/moderate; transdiagnostic meta). ACT for worry via acceptance/defusion (moderate–strong).

column2: Overestimating probability/cost of negative outcomes; interpreting sensations/events as dangerous.
column3: Worst-case predictions; urgency; avoidance; amplified pain-related distress.
column4: Fn: motivates protective action; reduces ambiguity. ST: “at least I’m prepared.”
column5: Anxiety escalation; functional impairment; chronic pain disability links in pain literature.
column6: Learned from adverse events; modeling; cognitive schemas; hyperarousal.
column7: Prominent in pain and anxiety samples; varies widely by context and measurement.
column8: Moderate–strong (pain catastrophizing evidence strong; broader cognitive bias predictive effects smaller but present).
column9: CBT cognitive restructuring + exposure/behavioral experiments (strong for anxiety; moderate for pain contexts). Acceptance-based approaches in pain (moderate).

column2: Rigid “either/or” evaluation (success/failure; good/bad).
column3: “If not perfect, worthless”; relapse -> “blown it”; extreme standards; moralized self-judgment.
column4: Fn: reduces ambiguity; simplifies decisions; protects identity via certainty. ST: clarity/decisiveness.
column5: Emotional volatility; binge-restrict cycles in eating/behavior change contexts; relationship polarization.
column6: Trait cognitive style; perfectionism; shame conditioning; social evaluation sensitivity.
column7: Scoping work suggests broad relevance; direct prevalence estimates are uncommon.
column8: Emerging–moderate (construct widely used; measurement and causal evidence uneven).
column9: CBT cognitive restructuring; behavioral experiments; graded exposure to “good enough” (moderate).

column2: Systematic bias to interpret ambiguity as threat/rejection or to infer others’ negative intent.
column3: “They think I’m incompetent”; hostile attribution; selective recall; post-event processing.
column4: Fn: early warning; social threat detection. ST: feels protective/realistic.
column5: Social avoidance, conflict escalation, loneliness; self-fulfilling prophecies via defensive behavior.
column6: Attachment insecurity; trauma; learned social threat expectancies; cognitive schemas.
column7: Present across anxiety/depression; prospective meta effects are small but reliable.
column8: Moderate (meta-analytic support for interpretation bias; smaller longitudinal prediction).
column9: CBT cognitive restructuring; social experiments; some cognitive bias modification variants (moderate).

column2: Persistent harsh self-evaluation and self-attacking standards.
column3: “Inner critic” self-talk; self-punishment; discounting success; high shame reactivity.
column4: Fn: error prevention, social rank protection; motivates effort via threat. ST: temporary drive/avoidance of rejection.
column5: Predicts poorer psychotherapy outcomes; linked to multiple symptom domains and interpersonal problems.
column6: Developmental criticism/conditional acceptance; insecure attachment; perfectionism schemas.
column7: Particularly salient in eating disorders and clinical samples; cross-diagnostic associations documented.
column8: Strong (systematic evidence on outcome prediction; change mechanisms).
column9: Compassion-focused therapy reduces self-criticism (moderate; meta suggests benefit but quality limits). Self-compassion-related interventions reduce self-criticism (moderate; meta).

column2: Global negative self-evaluation (“I am bad”) often tied to moral/social threat.
column3: Concealment; withdrawal; anger/defensiveness; compulsive repair or avoidance.
column4: Fn: signals social danger; attempts to prevent rejection; promotes appeasement. ST: reduces exposure risk.
column5: Strong links to broad psychopathology and depression; inhibits disclosure and help-seeking.
column6: Trauma, chronic criticism, stigmatization; cultural norms around honor/conformity.
column7: Trauma-related shame shows moderate associations with psychopathology in meta-analysis.
column8: Strong for association; intervention evidence moderate.
column9: Trauma-focused CBT and related therapies reduce PTSD symptoms (strong). CFT targets shame/self-criticism (emerging–moderate).

column2: Inflexible high standards with self-worth contingent on achievement.
column3: Overpreparing; checking; avoidance/procrastination; intolerance of “good enough.”
column4: Fn: reduces failure risk; secures approval/status. ST: perceived competence/control.
column5: Burnout, anxiety, depression; treatment resistance; relationship strain.
column6: Conditional acceptance; performance cultures; cognitive rigidity; self-criticism.
column7: Transdiagnostic risk and maintenance factor across disorders in meta-analytic work.
column8: Strong (transdiagnostic links + intervention meta-analyses).
column9: CBT for perfectionism reduces perfectionism and symptoms (moderate–strong). Self-compassion interventions may reduce perfectionistic self-attack (emerging–moderate).

column2: Persistent belief one’s success is undeserved; fear of being exposed as a fraud.
column3: Discounting achievements; overworking; avoidance of opportunities; reassurance seeking.
column4: Fn: protects against social “status loss”; pushes achievement striving. ST: vigilance, preparation.
column5: Burnout, anxiety/depression comorbidity, reduced satisfaction; often self-perpetuating.
column6: Perfectionism + contingent self-worth; stereotype threat/stigma contexts; critical environments.
column7: Prevalence estimates range widely (9–82%) depending on instrument/cutoffs; higher in some minority groups per review.
column8: Moderate (prevalence review strong; treatment trials limited).
column9: CBT skills for cognitive distortions; self-compassion training (emerging–moderate). Coaching: values-based goal setting + behavioral experiments (evidence extrapolated from CBT/behavior change).

column2: Self-esteem fluctuates based on approval, status, or performance domains.
column3: People-pleasing; compulsive comparison; attention/validation seeking; sensitivity to feedback.
column4: Fn: maintains belonging/status; reduces rejection threat. ST: momentary uplift via approval.
column5: Instability; vulnerability to depression/anxiety; relationship strain from self-validation goals.
column6: Conditional acceptance; attachment insecurity; sociometer dynamics; cultural/status pressures.
column7: Empirical links across contexts; formal prevalence estimates uncommon.
column8: Moderate (strong theory base; mixed causal evidence).
column9: Self-compassion interventions reduce self-criticism and may buffer contingency (moderate). Values-based ACT approaches reduce fusion with evaluation (moderate–strong).

column2: Attempts to prevent distress by increasing certainty/control.
column3: Overplanning; checking; micromanaging; reassurance; avoidance of ambiguity.
column4: Fn: reduces uncertainty distress. ST: immediate calm/order.
column5: Maintains anxiety; rigidity; interpersonal conflict; reduced adaptability.
column6: Temperament (harm avoidance), learning history, family modeling, trauma exposure.
column7: Transdiagnostic construct across anxiety/depression; effect sizes vary.
column8: Strong (meta-analytic IU evidence; transdiagnostic framing).
column9: CBT targeting IU (worry exposure, uncertainty training) (moderate–strong). ACT acceptance/defusion (moderate–strong).

column2: Expectation that actions won’t change outcomes; reduced effort and agency.
column3: “Why try?”; passivity; resignation; missed opportunities; depressive shutdown.
column4: Fn: conserves energy; avoids repeated failure/negative affect. ST: relief from striving.
column5: Depression maintenance; increased suicidality risk when hopelessness high; impaired recovery.
column6: Uncontrollable stress exposure; trauma; invalidating environments; attributional style (hopelessness theory).
column7: Hopelessness is a longitudinal risk factor for suicidal outcomes in meta-analysis (clinical utility modest but consistent).
column8: Strong theoretical basis; moderate–strong empirical (esp. hopelessness).
column9: Behavioral activation (increase reinforcement/agency) (strong). CBT for attributional style/problem-solving (moderate).

column2: Down-regulating by pushing down emotional expression/experience.
column3: “I’m fine”; reduced facial affect; difficulty sharing; delayed reactions; somatic tension.
column4: Fn: avoids conflict/shame; maintains social acceptability. ST: prevents escalation short-term.
column5: Associated with poorer affective outcomes on average; cultural moderation matters.
column6: Socialization; family rules; threat of rejection; trauma contexts.
column7: Cultural variability is well-documented in emotion regulation meta-analyses.
column8: Moderate (associations robust; causality and context dependence).
column9: Skills training in emotion identification/communication; CBT/DBT emotion regulation (moderate–strong). Couple interventions focusing on safer expression (moderate).

column2: Difficulty modulating emotion intensity; rapid action to escape affect.
column3: Explosive conflict; self-harm urges; substance coping; risky behaviors; interpersonal instability.
column4: Fn: immediate affect relief; regains control quickly. ST: downshifts unbearable states.
column5: Chronic instability; relationship rupture; self-harm risk; service overuse.
column6: Invalidating/traumatic environments; temperamental reactivity; attachment disruption.
column7: Central in BPD framing; varies across diagnoses.
column8: Strong (DBT evidence for suicidal/self-harm outcomes; guideline recognition of emotion regulation impairment).
column9: DBT reduces self-directed violence (strong for suicidal/self-harm). Schema therapy effective for personality disorders (moderate–strong).

column2: Persistent physiological threat activation and vigilance.
column3: Startle, scanning, irritability; sleep disturbance; avoidance; “always on edge.”
column4: Fn: danger detection; prevents re-harm. ST: readiness.
column5: Exhaustion; relationship conflict; avoidance increases; can sustain PTSD/anxiety loops.
column6: Trauma exposure; chronic stress; conditioning; insecure attachment.
column7: Particularly relevant in trauma-related conditions (PTSD frameworks).
column8: Strong (trauma clinical frameworks; guideline-supported treatments).
column9: Trauma-focused CBT and related trauma therapies (strong). Skills for arousal regulation (breathing, grounding) as adjuncts (moderate).

column2: Defensive immobilization or detachment during/after threat.
column3: “Blanking out”; time loss; numbness; immobility; derealization; shutdown during conflict.
column4: Fn: last-resort survival response; reduces pain/awareness. ST: protects during overwhelming threat.
column5: PTSD severity/prognosis impacts; functional impairment; difficulties integrating trauma memory; relational disengagement.
column6: Trauma exposure, especially interpersonal; prior dissociation; high perceived inescapability.
column7: Tonic immobility predicts poorer PTSD treatment response in some studies; dissociation plays transdiagnostic mediator roles in meta-analysis.
column8: Moderate–strong (for associations; mechanisms complex).
column9: Trauma-focused therapies + stabilization/grounding; phased treatment when dissociation severe (moderate–strong; guideline-aligned).

column2: Alternating overexertion and collapse/avoidance (often pain/fatigue contexts).
column3: “Good day” overdoing -> flare -> days of rest; inconsistent routines; guilt-driven bursts.
column4: Fn: preserves identity/role; captures opportunity on “good days.” ST: short-term achievement/normalcy.
column5: Worsened fatigue/pain disability; mood impact; reinforces fear-avoidance or endurance cycles.
column6: Reinforcement learning around symptoms; perfectionism; beliefs about self-worth and productivity.
column7: Documented in chronic pain/fatigue populations; pacing evidence varies by condition and implementation.
column8: Moderate (systematic review/meta in chronic pain; intervention evidence mixed).
column9: Activity pacing with graded progression can reduce fatigue/distress in CFS (moderate). CBT/rehabilitation pacing frameworks (emerging).

column2: Underestimating time/costs; focusing on “inside view” best-case plans.
column3: Unrealistic timelines; repeated “this time will be different”; deadline crises; overcommitment.
column4: Fn: maintains optimism/self-image; reduces anticipatory anxiety. ST: motivation boost.
column5: Chronic lateness, stress, reputational harm; intensifies procrastination.
column6: Cognitive bias toward inside view; limited use of base rates; perfectionism.
column7: Very common; not typically measured as prevalence but as bias magnitude in experiments.
column8: Strong (classic experimental evidence; replicated lines).
column9: “Outside view” base-rate planning; implementation intentions (moderate–strong). Mental contrasting with implementation intentions (MCII) improves goal attainment (moderate).

column2: Having goals but failing to translate into consistent action.
column3: Repeated “starting Monday”; reliance on motivation; inconsistent habits; avoidance when mood low.
column4: Fn: preserves aspirational identity without immediate cost. ST: hope/relief.
column5: Stagnation; self-trust erosion; shame cycles; opportunity costs.
column6: Executive load, low self-efficacy, value conflicts, stress.
column7: Widespread; measured in behavior change research as consistent phenomenon.
column8: Strong (behavior change meta-analytic base).
column9: Implementation intentions meta support (strong). Behavioral activation principles for mood-dependent inertia (strong).

column1: Identity rigidity / fixed mindset / self-concept instability
column2: Treating traits/emotions as unchangeable (“this is just who I am”).
column3: Avoids challenge; strong defensiveness; “label locking”; shame after setbacks.
column4: Fn: preserves coherence; reduces uncertainty. ST: protects from failure feelings.
column5: Higher distress; reduced learning and help-seeking; persistence of symptoms.
column6: Developmental beliefs about malleability; family/school messages; trauma.
column7: Meta-analysis links growth mindsets with lower distress; effects vary by context and intervention type.
column8: Moderate (associations solid; intervention impacts can be small and context-dependent).
column9: Growth mindset interventions (emerging–moderate). CBT/ACT to shift fusion with identity narratives (moderate).

column2: Repeating familiar relational dynamics (often harmful) due to learned schemas and attachment.
column3: Choosing similar partners; tolerating mistreatment; reenacting caregiver roles; self-sabotaging closeness.
column4: Fn: familiarity; attempts to “master” old pain; maintains coherent self-narrative. ST: predictability.
column5: Elevated risk of revictimization; chronic relationship distress; trauma reinforcement.
column6: Childhood maltreatment; insecure attachment; maladaptive schemas and emotion dysregulation as mediators.
column7: CM increases adult victimization risk; mediator models under active study.
column8: Moderate (strong correlational/systematic review base; causal pathways complex).
column9: Trauma-focused therapies; schema therapy for entrenched patterns (moderate–strong for personality pathology).

column2: One partner escalates for closeness; the other distances for safety/autonomy.
column3: Criticism or pleading -> withdrawal/stonewalling; protest behaviors; escalating bids.
column4: Fn: pursuer seeks reassurance/connection; withdrawer seeks arousal reduction. ST: temporary stabilization.
column5: Lower satisfaction; entrenched insecurity; reduced repair attempts; escalation into negative reciprocity.
column6: Attachment anxiety/avoidance; emotion regulation differences; family-of-origin conflict templates.
column7: Demand-withdraw predicts lower relationship functioning in meta-analysis; attachment insecurity correlates with lower satisfaction.
column8: Strong (robust relationship research + meta-analyses).
column9: Couple therapy/systemic interventions have evidence across adult mental health contexts (moderate). Attachment-oriented couple approaches widely used; evidence varies by model.

column2: Negative acts invite negative counteracts, creating an upward spiral.
column3: Criticism -> defensiveness -> contempt -> shutdown; tit-for-tat conflict; repair failure.
column4: Fn: self-protection, signaling boundaries. ST: venting, regaining power.
column5: Relationship distress; aggression risk; chronic insecurity; reduced positive interaction ratio.
column6: Stress load, poor repair modeling, attachment threat, emotion dysregulation.
column7: Common in distressed couples; exact prevalence hard to estimate due to measurement differences.
column8: Moderate (strong observational base; RCT evidence depends on treatment model).
column9: Skills-based couple therapy and systemic interventions (moderate). Emotion regulation skills (DBT-informed) for conflict escalation (moderate).

column2: Inhibiting needs/feelings to maintain attachment or avoid conflict.
column3: Agreeing while resentful; indirect communication; “walking on eggshells”; over-accommodation.
column4: Fn: prevents rejection/conflict. ST: reduces immediate interpersonal threat.
column5: Medium correlation with depression in meta-analysis; relationship dissatisfaction and loss of authenticity.
column6: Gendered/cultural role norms; criticism/invalidating families; anxious attachment.
column7: Meta-analysis shows r≈0.39 with depression; moderators include age, sexual orientation, education, and cultural context.
column8: Strong (for association), moderate (for causal direction).
column9: Assertiveness/boundary skills; CBT interpersonal experiments; couple therapy when relationally maintained (moderate).

column1: Unspoken contracts / unmet expectations / ideal-standards mismatch
column2: Implicit “rules” for closeness/fairness that are unshared and violated.
column3: Chronic disappointment; “you should know”; scorekeeping; passive resentment.
column4: Fn: maintains hope for reciprocal care without vulnerability of asking. ST: avoids direct negotiation discomfort.
column5: Stable dissatisfaction; conflict cycles; erosion of trust and goodwill.
column6: Attachment insecurity; family norms about needs; low relational communication skills.
column7: Studied via “ideal standards” and unmet expectations; not typically expressed as population prevalence.
column8: Emerging–moderate (solid correlational research; less standardized as a single construct).
column9: Explicit expectation negotiation; structured communication; couple therapy/systemic interventions (moderate).

column1: Maladaptive support seeking: co-rumination & reassurance-driven bonding
column2: Excessive problem-talk that increases closeness but sustains negative affect and helplessness.
column3: Friends/partners repeatedly rehash problems; reassurance cycles; dependence on validation.
column4: Fn: creates intimacy and certainty. ST: social soothing, belonging.
column5: Increases depressive symptoms risk (small but reliable correlations; stress generation pathways).
column6: Anxious attachment; limited emotion regulation repertoire; social reinforcement of complaint bonding.
column7: Meta-analysis (66 studies; N≈27,794) finds small positive association with depressive symptoms; weaker in adolescents than undergrads/adults.
column8: Moderate (meta-analytic support; effect sizes smaller than rumination).
column9: Coaching/therapy: shift from co-rumination to solution-focused + values/action; interpersonal boundaries; CBT skills for reassurance reduction (emerging–moderate).

column2: Habitual scanning of others’ status, outcomes, or traits to evaluate one’s own standing, with self-worth, motivation, and affect shifting in response to perceived relative position.
column3: Checking metrics (likes, promotions, income, attractiveness); upward comparison that deflates mood; downward comparison for relief; compulsive profile viewing; envy/resentment spikes after exposure to high performers.
column4: Fn: rapid self-evaluation; situational benchmarking; guides goal direction in status-relevant environments. ST: momentary motivation, relief via downward comparison, sense-making of social hierarchies.
column5: Contingent self-worth; chronic dissatisfaction; envy and shame; risk-avoidant or overstriving behavior; burnout; impaired intrinsic motivation; depressive symptoms.
column6: Status-salient environments; conditional approval based on performance; social media exposure; perfectionistic standards; identity fusion with achievement or appearance.
column7: Near-universal process; intensity elevated in adolescence/young adulthood and in highly competitive or visibility-driven domains.
column8: Moderate–Strong (robust experimental mood effects; longitudinal links to depression, self-esteem, and eating pathology).
column9: CBT cognitive restructuring of comparison interpretations; self-compassion training (moderate–strong); values-based action and attentional retraining (ACT; moderate); reducing comparison cues / stimulus control (behavioral).

column2: Heightened expectation and rapid detection of criticism, exclusion, or negative evaluation, followed by strong affective reactivity and defensive behavioral responses.
column3: Overpreparing or avoiding evaluative situations; reassurance seeking; defensive withdrawal after mild feedback; anger or shutdown after perceived slights; mind-reading for disapproval.
column4: Fn: anticipatory protection against social threat; attempts to prevent humiliation or exclusion. ST: temporary sense of control; threat detection feels adaptive; mobilizes performance effort.
column5: Relationship strain; misinterpretation of neutral cues; avoidance of growth opportunities; self-fulfilling rejection cycles; social anxiety and depressive symptoms.
column6: Histories of inconsistent or critical caregiving; peer rejection or bullying; contingent acceptance; high shame sensitivity; attachment insecurity.
column7: Continuously distributed trait; elevated in social anxiety, borderline features, and individuals with chronic interpersonal stress.
column8: Moderate–Strong (prospective prediction of relationship outcomes and depression; well-validated experimental paradigms).
column9: CBT for social anxiety (strong); exposure to evaluative situations (strong); mentalization-based approaches (moderate); compassion-focused therapy for shame reactivity (moderate).

column2: Reduction in activity, effort, and reward-seeking in response to low mood or anticipated failure, which decreases positive reinforcement and further deepens anhedonia and passivity.
column3: Staying in bed; canceling plans; task avoidance due to low energy or hopelessness; loss of previously reinforcing activities; narrowed routine.
column4: Fn: energy conservation; avoidance of perceived failure or overwhelm. ST: temporary relief from effort demands; reduced exposure to stressors.
column5: Loss of reinforcement; worsening mood; anhedonia; functional impairment; increased rumination; maintenance of depressive episodes.
column6: Low reinforcement environments; learned helplessness; repeated failure experiences; chronic stress; fatigue and sleep disruption; negative expectancy bias.
column7: Core maintenance process in major depression; also present in burnout and chronic stress states.
column8: Strong (behavioral activation RCTs; reinforcement–mood longitudinal data; mediation evidence).
column9: Behavioral activation (strong); activity scheduling with graded task assignment (strong); CBT targeting hopelessness and expectancy (strong); exercise as adjunct (moderate–strong).
